data.title: ASX Small-Cap Stocks to Watch
data.description:
Investors are likely to keep an eye on the following small-cap stocks listed on the Australian Securities Exchange.

Piche Resources

Piche Resources received results from its reverse circulation drilling program at the Ashburton project in Western Australia, confirming and upgrading previously reported high-grade uranium intersections.

Antares Metals

Antares Metals completed a geophysical work program on the Conglomerate Creek project in Western Australia, identifying several target areas for future follow-up. A further 3D investigation is underway to better understand the depth and scale of the targets.

The Calmer Co. International

The Calmer Co. International secured a FJ$2.6 million loan from Fiji Development Bank to acquire its Navua facility.

Loyal Lithium

Loyal Lithium is set to expand its North American lithium-led battery minerals and technology business beyond hard rock lithium exploration. The strategy will focus on US minerals and supply chain technologies for a dynamic electrical grid while maintaining its North American hard rock lithium assets.

Enova Mining

Enova Mining reported drill results at CODA North in Brazil, revealing multiple intercepts exceeding 15% titanium dioxide associated with rare earth element mineralization.

data.title: REA Looks Unlikely to Take Near-Term Tilt at Rightmove — Market Talk
data.description: The retirement of REA Group CEO Owen Wilson suggests to Citi analyst Siraj Ahmed that the Australian real-estate advertiser is unlikely to make another near-term takeover tilt at U.K. counterpart Rightmove. Ahmed points out that the six-month restriction period that followed REA's unsuccessful move for Rightmove expires in late March. Wilson is scheduled to retire in 2025. Ahmed writes in a note that he does not see guidance for higher costs as a big negative given that the increase is revenue-related. Citi has a buy rating and A$230.00 target price on the News Corp-controlled classifieds group. Shares are down 2.3% at A$246.02. News Corp. owns Dow Jones & Co., publisher of this newswire and The Wall Street Journal. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Safehold Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Safehold Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 36 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, identical to the same quarter last year. The mean expectation of nine analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 36 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 34 cents to 38 cents per share.

  • Revenue fell 10.8% to $91.87 million from a year ago; analysts expected $92.15 million.

  • Safehold Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 36 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $26.04 million.

  • Safehold Inc shares had fallen by 10.4% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 1.2% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, one analyst negatively revised an earnings estimate

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 7 "strong buy" or "buy," 5 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Safehold Inc is $25.00

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data February 5 at 11:40 p.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: EZCORP Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• EZCORP Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 42 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 36 cents. The mean expectation of three analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 37 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 35 cents to 39 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 6.7% to $320.17 million from a year ago; analysts expected $322.83 million.

  • EZCORP Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 40 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $31.02 million.

  • EZCORP Inc shares had fallen by 1.2% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 2% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, one analyst negatively revised an earnings estimate

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy," 1 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for EZCORP Inc is $15.00

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data February 5 at 11:41 p.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. UPS Raises Quarterly Dividend to $1.64 per Share
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

UPS is increasing its quarterly dividend to $1.64 per share.

The package-delivery company on Wednesday said its board of directors approved raising its quarterly payout to shareholders to $1.64 from $1.63 a share.

The new payout, equal to $6.56 annually, represents a yield of 5.9%, based on the company's closing price of $111.90.

The dividend will be paid on Feb. 18.

Last week, Finance Chief Brian Dykes said during the company's earnings call that UPS was planning to pay out around $5.5 billion in dividends in 2025, subject to board approval.

Write to Sabela Ojea at sabela.ojea@wsj.com

data.title: Nikkei May Rise as Concerns About U.S. Tariffs Ease — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about trade friction between U.S. and its trading partners recede. Nikkei futures are up 0.5% at 38950 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 152.66, compared with 153.27 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments over U.S. tariffs as well as domestic earnings. Itochu and Fujifilm Holdings are set to announce their quarterly earnings later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.1% to 38831.48 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

data.title: Mixed China Demand Signals for Penfolds Wine — Market Talk
data.description: Goldman Sachs analysts see mixed signals on Chinese demand for Treasury Wine Estates' Penfolds brand. Scanning e-commerce shopping channels for clues, they see the February price of Penfolds Bin 389 wine sitting close to where it was before the Lunar New Year celebrations. This is 17% lower than the prior month's tracking price and points to potentially weaker demand, they write in a note. However, the price of Bin 407 wine has increased on pre-Lunar New Year levels and is consistent with the prior month. They reckon this as evidence of strong retail demand. GS has a buy rating and A$13.00 target price on Treasury shares, which are up 0.75% at A$10.73 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1845 ET - Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about trade friction between U.S. and its trading partners recede. Nikkei futures are up 0.5% at 38950 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 152.66, compared with 153.27 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments over U.S. tariffs as well as domestic earnings. Itochu and Fujifilm Holdings are set to announce their quarterly earnings later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.1% to 38831.48 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1734 ET - Air New Zealand looks a good bet to achieve the upper end of 1H profit guidance following a solid finish to the period, Macquarie says. Revenue per available seat kilometer, a key performance metric for airlines, rose 0.7% in 1H when currency swings and travel credits are excluded. That reversed a fall in RASK through November. Air New Zealand previously signaled expectations for a 1H pretax profit of NZ$120 million-NZ$160 million. "With a strong December set of operating statistics, we expect Air New Zealand to be at the upper end of this range," Macquarie says. "While 2H has challenges, we see stabilization rather than any further deterioration." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1558 ET - Treasury yields fall again, with the 10-year dropping below 4.5% for the first time since Dec. 18, as relatively soft economic indicators retake center stage and tariff-related inflation fears ebb. ADP jobs report was stronger than expected, but the trade deficit was wider than forecast and services PMI surprisingly fell. Weekly jobless claims tomorrow are expected to indicate increasing layoffs and slowing job creation is forecast for Friday's payrolls report. The 10-year falls 0.090 percentage point to 4.421% and the two-year declines 0.030 to 4.183% (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1551 ET - The Bank of Mexico is expected to speed up its pace of monetary easing with a 50 basis-point cut Thursday in its benchmarkinterest rate to 9.5%, Citi says. Of the 35 analysts in Citi's latest survey, 27 expect a half-percentage point reduction and seven foresee a fifth consecutive quarter-point cut. Mexico's agreement with President Trump to hold off placing tariffs on Mexican goods at least until March is seen giving the central bank room to make the larger cut, especially as inflation has continued to ease and economic growth to slow, including a contraction in 4Q. The benchmark interest rate is forecast to end the year at 8.5%, according to the survey. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1522 ET - Corporate spreads are tight, but could get tighter, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He likes high-yield U.S. corporate credit because he sees yields as likely to lower and prices to increase. "The quality of the high-yield bond market has improved significantly," he says. Triick adds that the high-yield landscape now has more bonds rated BB and less rated C, reflecting shrinking risk in the asset class. "So there is room for high yield spreads to go a little bit tighter from here." That said, Triick sees more room for gains in mortgage-backed securities than in corporate bonds. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1516 ET - The Fed probably wants short-term yields below 4% with longer-term ones well above it, says Clayton Triick, head of portfolio management of public strategies at Angel Oak. In Wall Street parlance, policymakers would like the yield curve to steepen. "A steeper curve [is] good for growth, for debt growth, good for banks, and good for the economy." It would also be good for bonds in the front-end of the yield curve, which are mostly above 4%. Their prices would rise if yields fell. "I think there's a good chance that bonds could can do quite well" as the curve steepens, Triick says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1500 ET - Fed governor Michelle Bowman notes that Treasury-market liquidity has been a steady source of concern for years, and suggests that regulatory reforms for banks could ease the problem. Speaking at a banking conference in Topeka, Kan., Bowman notes that when bank balance sheets expand, regulatory leverage ratios can become a binding constraint, even if the bank is investing in safe assets. "When constrained in this way, bank-affiliated primary dealers may pull back on market intermediation activities," Bowman says. "Where we can take proactive regulatory measures to ensure that primary dealers have adequate balance sheet capacity to intermediate Treasury markets, we should do so." She suggests amending the leverage ratio and G-SIB surcharge regulations for big U.S. banks could help. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1458 ET - Investors grew worried about inflation during the trade war flare-up, but the U.S. housing market is likely to counter price pressures elsewhere, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects new-home supply to increase, cooling prices and helping keep the Fed on track to cut rates. Housing market dynamics also make investing in mortgages an attractive option for fixed-income investors. Triick is particularly bullish on higher-yielding mortgages not backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. "You are getting elevated yields and the fundamentals of housing are still really strong," he says. A drop on house prices could increase defaults, but Triick says it is unlikely to happen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1454 ET - First-term Trump tariffs didn't matter much for aggregate inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says, speaking at a conference in Detroit. But he warns the impact could be worse this time around. As companies adapted their supply chains, they likely shifted "all the easiest things out of China," Goolsbee says, leaving behind production inputs that are harder to move. Such trade will remain vulnerable to tariffs because there are no ready substitutes, Goolsbee says: "Tariffs on intermediate goods raise the costs of production for domestic manufacturers of other goods, so the inflation impact is unlikely to stay only in the directly affected products." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1451 ET - Bond prices look "pretty attractive" this year in the U.S., Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects the Fed to trim interest rates by 25 or 50 basis points in 2025, as inflation remains sticky but is unlikely to accelerate, he says. Triick argues that yields are elevated and likely to decline in coming months, in which case bond prices will be going up. "It is probably going to be a really good year for bonds," he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1447 ET - Tariffs would likely complicate the Fed's job in responding to further stubbornness in inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says. Speaking at a conference in Detroit, Goolsbee says that depending on White House policy, the Fed will have to determine whether tariff-induced price increases are a one-time adjustment or reflect a deeper pattern before reacting. "If we see inflation rising or progress stalling in 2025, the Fed will be in the difficult position of trying to figure out if the inflation is coming from overheating or if it's coming from tariffs," Goolsbee says. "That distinction will be critical for deciding when or even if the Fed should act." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1333 ET - Global sustainable bond issuance is expected to remain steady in 2025, at about $1 trillion, according to S&P Global Ratings in a report. The agency says "with green bonds and supranational entities in the lead, the sustainable bond market seems to have returned to its roots." Corporate green bond issuance is now well established, S&P says. However, issuance fell in 2024 to about 11% of the global total, from 13% in 2023. More issuance in low-income and emerging economies is expected to address the climate finance gap, S&P says, but adds "we see a risk that macroeconomic and political factors could stifle further growth." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1845 ET - Goldman Sachs analysts see mixed signals on Chinese demand for Treasury Wine Estates' Penfolds brand. Scanning e-commerce shopping channels for clues, they see the February price of Penfolds Bin 389 wine sitting close to where it was before the Lunar New Year celebrations. This is 17% lower than the prior month's tracking price and points to potentially weaker demand, they write in a note. However, the price of Bin 407 wine has increased on pre-Lunar New Year levels and is consistent with the prior month. They reckon this as evidence of strong retail demand. GS has a buy rating and A$13.00 target price on Treasury shares, which are up 0.75% at A$10.73 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1841 ET - The retirement of REA Group CEO Owen Wilson suggests to Citi analyst Siraj Ahmed that the Australian real-estate advertiser is unlikely to make another near-term takeover tilt at U.K. counterpart Rightmove. Ahmed points out that the six-month restriction period that followed REA's unsuccessful move for Rightmove expires in late March. Wilson is scheduled to retire in 2025. Ahmed writes in a note that he does not see guidance for higher costs as a big negative given that the increase is revenue-related. Citi has a buy rating and A$230.00 target price on the News Corp-controlled classifieds group. Shares are down 2.3% at A$246.02. News Corp. owns Dow Jones & Co., publisher of this newswire and The Wall Street Journal. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1829 ET - Ford would be able to manage a few weeks of President Trump's proposed tariffs given the rate and flow of its products, CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts. If they persisted, though, they would have a devastating impact, he says. The company already has plans to expand some of its U.S. plants to mitigate higher import costs, but if the proposed tariffs become a long-term reality, Ford would have to make some major strategy shifts, such as building all new plants in the U.S., Farley says. Shares fall 5.1% after hours. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1828 ET - Bell Potter junks its sell call on Platinum Asset Management, but says the trend for revenue and costs continues to look difficult. Platinum ended talks with Regal Partners over a takeover worth about A$0.90/share late last year. Its stock is currently below A$0.69. That ramps up pressure on Platinum's board to deliver more value than the approach it turned down, analyst Marcus Barnard says. "With revenue still falling, new growth initiatives yet to deliver and the shares trading well below Regal's offer, the company has a lot to prove," Bell Potter says. It moves to hold, from sell. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1821 ET - Golub Capital's credit and earnings performance will continue to outperform the industry over the next 12 to 24 months, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. The direct lender and private credit asset manager is now above target leverage, faster than Raymond James projected, and producing an attractive return on equity for a lower-than-average risk portfolio, the analysts say. Still, that is priced into shares at current levels, the analysts add. Shares closed down 2.3% to $15.31. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1817 ET - Ares Management expects that some of the policies of the new Trump administration will foster dealmaking, giving a further boost to what the private-credit fund manager sees as an already favorable market for investments, Ares CEO Michael Arougheti says during an earnings call. "We expect transaction activity to expand year-over-year," he says. "The new administration could provide some tailwinds for deployment, especially if an improved regulatory and antitrust environment enables more business growth and [mergers and acquisitions] activity in the market." Ares ended December with $133 billion in dry powder, or capital available to spend, a record for the firm. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1814 ET - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that President Trump's proposed tariffs could hurt the U.S. auto industry. "There's no question that tariffs at the 25% level from Canada and Mexico, if they're protracted, would have a huge impact on our industry, with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out and adverse effects on U.S. jobs," he says. Consumers would also face higher prices, he adds. Ford applauds Trump's administration for the agreements with Mexico and Canada earlier this week and says it is closely monitoring the situation in China. Shares fall 5.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1812 ET - Comet Ridge's signal that a final investment decision on its Mahalo natural-gas project in Australia won't happen this year contributes to Bell Potter cutting its price target by 19% to A$0.21/share. Still, analyst Stuart Howe remains bullish about Comet Ridge, highlighting that Mahalo is one of the few development-ready projects capable of delivering near-term gas supply to eastern Australia. Comet Ridge expects preliminary design and engineering work at Mahalo to continue through this year. Only when this is completed will it make a final investment decision, or FID, with partner Santos. "We expect Comet Ridge's majority equity positions in the Mahalo projects and a strong gas market outlook to provide support for development funding across debt, equity and gas prepayments," Bell Potter says. "However, we also acknowledge risks to FID timing." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1808 ET - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that the company believes President Trump and his administration, as well as other congressional leaders, are committed to strengthening the U.S. auto industry. "That is certainly our expectation, and we look forward to working with our leaders to make sure that that becomes a reality, because they understand and appreciate how vital our industry is to jobs, the economy, our national security and the communities across our country," he says. Shares fall 5.3% after the company says it expects profitability to shrink in 2025. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1803 ET - Centuria Office REIT's 1H result suggests to Bell Potter that asset valuations are nearing a trough. It highlights that valuations of 37% of Centuria Office REIT's portfolio increased or stabilized in 1H. "This, along with improving office transaction volumes in 1H, suggest book values are close to 'the bottom'," says analyst Connor Eldridge. Bell Potter now estimates an expansion of 40 basis points in Centuria Office REIT's 1H weighted average capitalization rate over the next 12 months. Evidence that asset values are finding a floor "will alleviate balance-sheet pressure," Bell Potter says. It notes that Centuria Office REIT had gearing of 42.9% at the end of June, above its 25%-35% target range. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1744 ET - Beach Energy's interim dividend falls short of market expectations, with Barrenjoey noting the payout doesn't align with the company's distribution policy. Beach declared an interim dividend of 3 Australian cents a share. While that was up 50% on last year it fell short of the 4 cents anticipated by the market. Analyst Dale Koenders points out that Beach reported free cash flow of A$431 million in 1H. "But this free cash flow then also implies a payout ratio of only 16%, below policy of 40-50%," Koenders says in his initial response to the result. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1739 ET - Zircon prices are likely to remain resilient, benefiting Australia's Iluka Resources, says Macquarie. It expects the zircon price to stay above US$1,730/ton in the medium term and then rise in 2029 when the market tips into a deficit. "We also highlight that our supply forecast does not include production rationalization by major producers, which happened during the last zircon price trough in 2016," Macquarie says. Demand trends look positive. The continued push by the Chinese government to ensure property developments are completed supports demand for ceramics, which represents some 50% of zircon consumption, Macquarie says. It keeps an outperform call on Iluka. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: KeePer LABO Expands in Tochigi with New Franchise Store Opening
data.description:
KeePer Technical Laboratory will open KeePer LABO Utsunomiya Tsuruta on March 12, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The 480.1 square meter store in Tochigi, which will be operated by Apec's Co., features nine coating booths and four washing spaces, allowing customers to observe the detailing process.

The store is KeePer LABO's 147th, and the 18th franchise location.

data.title: MicroStrategy, now ‘Strategy,’ records $670M net loss in Q4
data.description:
Bitcoin stacking firm Strategy — which has just rebranded from MicroStrategy — reported a net loss of $670.8 million for the fourth quarter as the firm stacked an additional 218,887 Bitcoin.

On Feb. 5, Strategy reported $120.7 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, marking a 3% year-on-year fall that missed analyst estimates by about $2 million.

The firm’s expenses for Q4 rose nearly 700% year-on-year to $1.1 billion as it started executing its “21/21 Plan” — targeting $42 billion in capital over the next three years, split equity and fixed-income securities — to buy more Bitcoin .

Strategy said it has already completed $20 billion of that $42 billion capital plan, fueling its Bitcoin buying spree largely through senior convertible notes and debt.

Strategy CEO and president Phong Le said the firm is already “significantly ahead” of its initial timeline and is “well-positioned to further enhance shareholder value by leveraging the strong support from institutional and retail investors for our strategic plan.”

The company’s Bitcoin holdings now sit at 471,107 Bitcoin, worth over $45 billion, the largest of any corporation in the world.

Strategy’s “BTC Yield” — a KPI representing the percentage change ratio between its Bitcoin and its Assumed Diluted Shares Outstanding — reached 74.3% in 2024, but the firm is lowering its target to 15% for 2025.

The firm also introduced the annual “BTC Gain” and “BTC $ Gain” metrics to better reflect the health of Strategy’s balance sheet.

BTC Gain represents the number of Bitcoin that it holds at the beginning of a period multiplied by the BTC Yield for such period, while BTC $ Gain represents the dollar value of the BTC Gain.

Strategy rebranded from MicroStrategy on Feb. 5 — which had been the firm’s name since it was founded as a business intelligence firm in November 1989 by executive chairman Michael Saylor.

Saylor has been the orchestrator behind the company’s Bitcoin investment strategy.

Strategy added the “₿” Bitcoin logo next to its new name to reflect its commitment to corporate Bitcoin adoption. Strategy will continue to offer business intelligence services.

Strategy (MSTR) fell 3.3% during the Feb. 5 trading day to $336.70 and has dropped another 0.72% in after-hours, Google Finance data shows.

Yaacov Nagar, a 10% owner of 374Water, sold a total of 228,039 shares of common stock on February 3rd and 4th, 2025. The sales were conducted at prices of $0.4942 and $0.4501 per share, respectively, totaling $111,994. Following these transactions, Nagar directly owns 36,545,756 shares of 374Water.

data.title: 2,200m Follow-up Drilling Complete at Tolmer Au-Ag Discovery
data.description:
Testing strike extensions near grades of 83.6 g/t Au and 312 g/t Ag

ADELAIDE, AUSTRALIA / ACCESS Newswire / February 5, 2025 / Barton Gold Holdings Limited

HIGHLIGHTS

  • Significant gold and silver assays from May - July 2024 RC drilling include assays of 4m @ 24.6 g/t Au and 82.8 g/t Ag from 95m depth (incl. 1m @ 83.6 g/t Au and 312 g/t Ag from 97m) [1]

  • Silver assays also remain outstanding for ~2,000m aircore (AC) drilling during November 2024 1

Barton Gold Holdings Limited (FRA:BGD3)(OTCQB:BGDFF) (Barton or Company) is pleased to confirm that further RC drilling has been completed at the Tarcoola Gold Project's (Tarcoola) Tolmer prospect in South Australia. 17 RC holes were drilled for a total of 2,230 metres completed. 1 Drilling targeted potential extensions of higher-grade quartz sulphide veining, and below November 2024 aircore (AC ) drilling which intersected gold but was unable to penetrate the underlying harder quartzite rock. 1

Figure 1 - Tolmer plan map with 2024 RC and AC drill collars, new RC drill collars, and key assays 1

Commenting on recent drilling at the Tolmer prospect, Barton MD Alexander Scanlon said:

"We are excited to quickly follow up drilling at the Tolmer prospect, having recently identified high-grade silver alongside the previously reported high-grade gold intercepts. We are also looking forward to receiving the results of further silver assays from our November 2024 aircore drilling, which are now underway."

Authorised by the Managing Director of Barton Gold Holdings Limited.

For further information, please contact:

  • Alexander Scanlon

    Managing Director

    a.scanlon@bartongold.com.au

    +61 425 226 649

  • Jade Cook

    Company Secretary

    cosec@bartongold.com.au

    +61 8 9322 1587

About Barton Gold

Barton Gold is an ASX, OTCQB and Frankfurt Stock Exchange listed Australian gold developer targeting future gold production of 150,000oz annually, with ~1.6Moz Au JORC Mineral Resources (52.3Mt @ 0.94 g/t Au), multiple advanced exploration projects and brownfield mines, and 100% ownership of the only regional gold mill in the renowned central Gawler Craton of South Australia. *

Competent Persons Statement & Previously Reported Information

The information in this announcement that relates to the historic Exploration Results and Mineral Resources as listed in the table below is based on, and fairly represents, information and supporting documentation prepared by the Competent Person whose name appears in the same row, who is an employee of or independent consultant to the Company and is a Member or Fellow of the Australasian Institute of Mining and Metallurgy (AusIMM), Australian Institute of Geoscientists (AIG) or a Recognised Professional Organisation (RPO). Each person named in the table below has sufficient experience which is relevant to the style of mineralisation and types of deposits under consideration and to the activity which he has undertaken to quality as a Competent Person as defined in the JORC Code 2012 (JORC).

data.title: Steris lowers annual profit forecast due to a stronger dollar
data.description:
Medical equipment maker Steris lowered its annual profit forecast on Wednesday, citing the impact of a stronger dollar.

The Dublin-based company lowered its fiscal 2025 adjusted profit to the range of $9.05 to $9.15 per share, from a prior forecast of $9.05 to $9.25 per share.

"This outlook does not reflect any potential impact from new tariffs." the company said.

Manufacturers of medical devices have been benefiting from elevated demand for elective surgical procedures in the United States, especially among older adults.

Steris, which produces surgical instruments, reported quarterly revenue of $1.37 billion, slightly below analysts' estimates of $1.38 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.

The healthcare segment of the company, which provides sterilization products to healthcare providers, saw its quarterly revenue rise 7% in the third quarter ended December 31, to $976.0 million.

On an adjusted basis, the company reported a profit of $2.32 per share, which was in line with analysts' expectations.

data.title: Digi International Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:

  • Revenue fell 2.1% to $103.87 million from a year ago; analysts expected $104.08 million.

  • Digi International Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 27 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $10.08 million.

  • Digi International Inc shares had risen by 0.5% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 2.6% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

  • The average consensus recommendation for the communications & networking peer group is also "buy"

Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Digi International Inc is $38.00

data.title: PEXA Revises Fiscal H1 Specific Items Outlook Amid AU$15 Million Investment Impairment
data.description:
PEXA Group has revised its specified items outlook for its fiscal first half to a range of AU$35 to AU$40 million from the previous outlook of AU$15 to AU$20 million, the company said in a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

The adjustment comes as the company expects to recognize a non-cash impairment charge of approximately AU$15 million in its fiscal H1 results, and there is no tax impact resulting from this change, per the filing.

The company also revised its income tax expense outlook to a range of AU$40 million to AU$45 million from AU$15 to AU$20 million.

data.title: Beam Dental to Close Pacific Smiles Takeover Offer After Acquiring 89.23% Voting Power
data.description:
Pacific Smiles Group said Beam Dental Bidco will close the offer period for its off-market takeover bid for Pacific Smiles on Feb. 11, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

Beam's voting power in Pacific Smiles stands at 89.23%, which gives it control of Pacific Smiles and sufficient voting power to approve special resolutions.

data.title: Did REA Group’s first-half result meet expectations?
data.description:
Key points:
  • REA Group's first-half FY25 result exceeded consensus on revenue, EBITDA, and NPAT, but dividend came in lower than expected, and expenses were marginally higher
  • Management reaffirmed double-digit yield growth but flagged rising costs, tough year-on-year comparisons, and higher-than-expected associate losses.
  • REA trades at a 109x P/E, its most expensive level in nearly four years, which may limit upside despite strong fundamentals

REA Group reported its first-half FY25 earnings, slightly ahead of consensus expectations. Here’s what you need to know.

1H25 Earnings Summary

1H25

% Chg

Consensus

% Dif

Revenue ($m)

873

20.3%

853.3

2.3%

Expenses ($m)

338

17.9%

338.6

-0.2%

EBITDA ($m)

521

22.3%

514.8

1.2%

NPAT ($m)

314

25.8%

307.4

2.1%

EPS (cps)

238

25.9%

232.1

2.5%

DPS (cps)

110

26.4%

114.9

-4.2%

Source: REA Group, VA Consensus

Management Comments

Healthy listings environment: "Vendors remained confident during the half with sales volumes consistently higher than the prior year, demonstrating the depth of demand, while buyers benefitted from more choice and some moderation in price growth."

Positive macro drivers: "Strong employment, high immigration levels and expectations for interest rate cuts in the first half of 2025 continue to support buyer demand and vendor confidence to list."

Strong start to the year but cycling high comps: "January National residential new Buy listings were up 3% YoY, with Sydney increasing by 5% and Melbourne declining by 2%. As previously flagged, YoY growth rates for the second half of the financial year will reflect very strong prior period listings volumes, particularly for Melbourne and Sydney."

FY25 outlook: "Our expectation for double-digit FY25 residential Buy yield growth remains unchanged ... We continue to target positive operating jaws in FY25. Low double-digit group core operating cost growth is now anticipated, compared to high single-digits previously."

Key Takeaways

REA delivered a strong result, slightly beating consensus across most key metrics. However, the dividend came in softer than expected, and expenses were marginally higher.

"This is a good result, with a solid start to 2H25 somewhat offset by higher operating cost guidance. Given the stock’s strong performance leading into the result, we expect it to trade in line with the market today," said E&P Capital analyst Entcho Raykovski.

It’s worth noting that REA is trading at its most expensive level in nearly four years, with a price-to-earnings ratio of 109. While the earnings were solid, the valuation remains a key concern.

Outlook and CEO Change

While REA’s guidance was broadly positive, a few headwinds stand out:

  • Higher costs: Core operating expenses are now expected to grow in the low double digits, versus prior guidance of high single digits.

  • Challenging YoY comparisons: 2H25 growth rates will be cycling an exceptionally strong prior period.

  • Associate losses: FY25 associate losses are now expected to be slightly higher YoY, compared to prior guidance of modestly lower losses.

  • CEO transition: CEO Owen Wilson announced plans to retire later this year

"While Owen is highly regarded, the strength of REA’s business model should sustain a CEO transition," said Raykovski.

Wilson currently owns approximately 68,800 REA shares (~$17 million), which is relatively modest compared to founder-led peers like Wisetech and Netwealth.

The Bottom Line

REA delivered a strong result, but with the stock trading at a premium, any weaknesses—such as higher costs and tough YoY comparisons—tend to get magnified. The company's fundamental momentum remains intact, but valuation concerns could weigh on near-term gains.

data.title: Australia's Beach Energy tumbles as results, production forecast miss the mark
data.description:
** Shares of Beach Energy fall 3.6% to A$1.46, heading for their strongest drop since December 9, if losses hold

** Oil and gas explorer fell as much as 4.6% earlier in the day, hitting lowest level since January 13

** Company posts HY underlying NPAT of A$237 million ($148.88 million), missing Citi's and Jarden's estimates

** Declares interim dividend of 3.0 Australian cents per share, missing a Jarden estimate of 6.0 AU cents per share

** Co narrows its FY25 production outlook to between 18.5 to 20.5 MMboe from earlier forecast of 17.5 to 21.5 MMboe

** Jarden analysts say they were expecting production forecast towards the upper end of the prior range

** Stock up 8.6% this year, as of last close

($1 = 1.5918 Australian dollars)

data.title: Toyota Motor Buys Back 15.6 Million Shares
data.description:
Toyota Motor repurchased 15.6 million shares of its common stock for a total of 45.59 billion yen between Jan. 6 and Jan. 31, under its ongoing buyback program, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The repurchase is part of a broader plan to buy back up to 530 million shares for a maximum of 1.2 trillion yen, running until April.

data.title: Platinum Asset Management Loses Bear, But Has Lots to Prove — Market Talk
data.description: Bell Potter junks its sell call on Platinum Asset Management, but says the trend for revenue and costs continues to look difficult. Platinum ended talks with Regal Partners over a takeover worth about A$0.90/share late last year. Its stock is currently below A$0.69. That ramps up pressure on Platinum's board to deliver more value than the approach it turned down, analyst Marcus Barnard says. "With revenue still falling, new growth initiatives yet to deliver and the shares trading well below Regal's offer, the company has a lot to prove," Bell Potter says. It moves to hold, from sell. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Correction to News Corp Earnings Rise Article
data.description: News Corp reported second-quarter earnings of 38 cents a share, and 27 cents a share a year ago. "News Corp Earnings Rise, Buoyed by Dow Jones and Digital Real Estate Segments — WSJ," at 4:53 p.m. ET, incorrectly said it reported per-share earnings of 40 cents and 28 cents, respectively.
